video_title: Penetration Testing For Beginners | Phases of Penetration Testing | Penetration Testing Tutorial

segments.text: Hello everyone and welcome to another exciting video on Cybersecurity by Idureka.
Today, we are diving into the world of penetration testing.
A critical practice that helps organization identify vulnerabilities before attackers do.
So in this video, we will explore penetration testing and why it is an essential part of
a robust cyber security strategy.
We will walk you through the entire process from the initial stages of gathering information
and scanning networks to the practical aspects of gaining access and maintaining it and
finally, analyzing the results.
So we will also discuss the different approaches to penetration testing including open box,
closed box, covert, external and internal methods while highlighting the variety of tools
that professionals use.
This range from reconnaissance and vulnerability scanning to specialized proxies and exploitation
frameworks and even post exploitation techniques for further analysis.
And to make it all clear and actionable, we will conclude with a live demonstration using
a vulnerable application, showing you how to exploit it and then compiling a comprehensive
appendiced report.
And before we begin, make sure to like, share and subscribe to Idureka's YouTube channel
and hit the bell icon to see updated on the latest content from Idureka.
Along with this, check out the certified ethical hacking course designed in partnership with
EasyConsult to elevate your cyber security skills.
This course offers a globally recognized CEH V13 AI certification and features 20 comprehensive
models that cover over 500 attack techniques enhanced by AI GPT tools.
You will gain hands-on experience through 221 practical labs and benefit from the round
the clock support provided by certified professionals.
And with access to exclusive e-courses where and an exam voucher from EasyConsult, this
course is your gateway to mastering ethical hacking and securing a rewarding career in cyber
defense.
So check out the course link given in the description box below.
Alright, now let us understand what is penetration testing.
Well, a penetration testing or pen testing is an authorized simulated attack on a computer
system designed to elevate its security.
In a pen test, security experts employ the same tools, techniques and procedures as real
world attackers do to identify vulnerabilities and demonstrate the potential business impact
of these weaknesses.
So this process helps organizations understand where the defenses may be lacking and where
improvements are needed.
Penetration testing not only reveals vulnerabilities but also offers several key benefits.
So let us quickly go over some of these advantages.
So first of the list, we have proactive vulnerability identification.
It helps you to uncover potential weaknesses before malicious actors can exploit them.
Next, risk mitigation, which reduces the likelihood and impact of cyber attacks, safeguarding
sensitive data.
And the next advantage is regulatory compliance.
We have industry standards and legal requirements avoiding penalties.
Next is the cost savings.
Prevent financial losses by addressing issues early and avoiding breaches.
And then, enhanced trust.
Build and maintain trust with customers and stakeholders by demonstrating robust security
practices.
And the next advantage is continuous improvement.
Gain insights that drive ongoing security enhancements and risk management.
Now let's dive into the phases of penetration testing each one building on the previous
to ensure a thorough security evaluation.
And the first phase is planning and reconnaissance.
So we start with planning and reconnaissance.
In this initial phase, the pen tester defines the scope and goals of the test.
Identifying which systems will be targeted and outlining the testing methods to be used.
It also involves gathering crucial intelligence such as network details, domain names and
mail server information to gain a clear understanding of how the target operates and where its vulnerability
might lie.
Now moving on to the next phase, which is scanning.
Once the groundwork is late, the next phase is scanning.
Here, the tested examines how the target application responds to various intrusions
attempts.
So this is a shift through both static and dynamic analysis.
Static analysis involves inspecting the application's code to predict its behavior.
While dynamic analysis absorbs the code in a running state, providing real-time insights
into its performance and weaknesses.
After scanning, the focus shifts to gaining access.
So in this stage, testers employ various web application attacks such as cross-site scripting,
SQL injection and the use of backdose to exploit discovered vulnerabilities.
By escalating privileges, intercepting traffic or even stealing data, they demonstrate the
potential impact these weaknesses could have on the system.
So the process then moves to maintaining access.
So the aim here is to see if vulnerability can be exploited to establish a persistent presence
within the system, mimicking advanced persistent threats.
So this phase is critical, as it shows how attacker might remain hidden for extended periods,
getting deeper access to sensitive information.
And finally, all the findings are brought together in the analysis phase.
The tester compiles a detailed report that outlines the specific vulnerability exploited,
the sensitive data access and the duration for which they were able to remain undetected.
So this comprehensive analysis provides organization with actionable insights to strengthen their
security posture.
Here, each phase plays an essential role in ensuring that the penetration testing process
is methodical and effective.
This ultimately helps organizations secure these systems against potential attacks.
Now let's explore the different methods of penetration testing, each offering a unique
perspective and evaluating a system security posture.
And the first method is open box penetration testing.
In open box penetration testing, the tester is provided with some information about the
target company's security setup.
This prior knowledge allows the tester to focus on specific areas of concern and design
targeted simulations that validate the effectiveness of existing security measures.
On the other hand, close box penetration testing often referred to as a single blind
test, limits the tester's information to only the target company's name.
And this approach challenges the tester to uncover vulnerabilities without any insight
to you, and closely replicating the conditions and external attacker would face.
And the next method is covert penetration testing.
In covert penetration testing, also known as a double blind test, the situation is taken
a step further.
Almost no one within the organization, including the IT and security teams, is aware that a test
is underway.
This method is particularly useful for simulating this tell the nature of real world attackers.
However, it is essential that the tester has documented details of the scope and rules
of engagement to ensure legal and ethical boundaries are maintained.
Another distinct method is external penetration testing, where the ethical hacker focuses
on the company's outward facing technology, such as its website and external network servers.
In some cases, the tester may not be permitted to enter the company's premises, meaning
the attacker is orchestrated remotely or even from a nearby location, mirroring the tactics
of a determined outsider.
And lastly, internal penetration testing involves conducting the assessment from within the company's
own network.
This method is invaluable for understanding the potential damage that could be inflected
by an incited threat or an attack who has already bypassed external defenses.
By simulating an internal breach, organizations can goch the effectiveness of their internal
controls and respond strategies.
So each method provides a distinct approach to identify vulnerabilities, ensuring that
organizations receive a comprehensive evaluation of their security landscape.
Now, there isn't a one tool for penetration testing.
An intelligent targets demand specialized approaches for task-like port scanning, application
scanning, Wi-Fi break-ins or directly breaching a network.
Broadly speaking, the tools used in penetration testing fall into five main categories.
The first category is reconnaissance tools, which are essentially for discovering network
host and open ports.
Tools like Enmap provides a detailed overview of a network's layout, while utilities like
Angry IP scanner help quickly identify life host and active services.
Next, vulnerability scanners comes into play.
These tools are designed to probe network services with applications and APIs for potential
security issues.
Tools like Nessus and OpenWaz can for known vulnerabilities, offering insights into where
defenses might be weak.
Then there is a proxy tools, which serve as a specialized web-prox or generate man in
the middle process.
Tools such as Burpsuit is widely used to intercept and analyze traffic, allowing testers to manipulate
requests and responses to uncover security flaws in web applications.
Exploitation tools form the fourth category.
These tools are used to leverage the vulnerabilities uncovered during earlier phases.
Made as Floyd, for example, is a well-known exploitation framework that enabled testers
to develop and execute exploits and effectively demonstrating how a breath might occur.
And finally, post-exploitation tools a crucial once-axis has been gained.
They allow testers to interact with compromised systems, maintain their food hold, and even
expand access to further illustrate the potential damage.
Tools like Kobalt Strike and PowerShell Empire exemplify this category, providing functionalities
that simulate the persistent threats seen in real-world attacks.
And each of these tool categories plays an integral role in the penetration testing process.
It ensures that every step from initial recognizance to post-exploitation is thoroughly executed
to evaluate and ultimately strengthen an organization's security posture.
Now that we have covered the theory behind penetration testing, so it's time to see
it in action.
So in this final segment, we will dive into a live demonstration by exploiting a vulnerable
application known as depth node.
We will walk you through the exploitation process step-by-step, showing you exactly how
vulnerabilities are identified and leveraged.

page.

Now confirm these changes by clicking OK.
And finally, start the virtual machine to launch depth node one and begin your penetration
testing demonstration.
Now that you have set up the target machine, so let's configure your attacker machine,
Kali Linux to begin the reconnaissance phase.
Now moving on to step 3.
Booting up our Kali Linux machine.

network adapter.

Once done, boot up your Kali Linux machine.
This system has fully loaded, open a terminal window to ensure that your machine has the
